title: NPS Public Water System Analysis
text:
Show R Code
library(leaflet)
library(dplyr)
library(sf)
library(htmltools)
library(readr)
library(sf)
library(flextable)
library(purrr)
library(ggplot2)
library(plotly)
library(scales) 
library(ggrepel)
library(stringr)

title: NPS Public Water System Analysis
text:
🌍 Why This Project Matters
Our National Parks are global treasures, welcoming over 300 million visitors annually alongside thousands of resident park rangers and support staff. While visitors marvel at towering redwoods or dramatic canyons, an invisible, highly complex web of Public Water Systems (PWS) quietly ensures public health, safety, and operational continuity.
Managing water in these landscapes is uniquely challenging: Infrastructure Age & Isolation: Many park systems operate in remote areas, far from municipal grids, relying on localized infrastructure that is difficult to maintain and monitor. Climate Vulnerability: From mountain springs drying up to surface water reservoirs suffering from algal blooms and wildfire-related sediment runoff, climate change is actively threatening these vital resources. Regulatory Oversight: Because these systems serve the public, they must strictly comply with the EPA’s Safe Drinking Water Act (SDWA).
This project bridges the gap between raw federal environmental databases and actionable asset management. By linking the National Park Service Water Supply Database (WSD) with the EPA’s Safe Drinking Water Information System (SDWIS), we can isolate, visualize, and prioritize the water systems that are most critical to park operations and public safety.

title: NPS Public Water System Analysis
text:
🎯 Identifying the “High-Priority” Systems
To effectively allocate resources, we categorize and prioritize these water systems based on three primary dimensions:

1. Scale of Human Impact (Population Served)
Not all water systems are built equal. We utilize an 11-category population scale to identify systems serving massive visitor hubs vs. small backcountry stations: High-Impact Systems (Class 5 to 11): Systems serving over 3,300 to upwards of 1 million people (such as primary hubs in Yosemite, Grand Canyon, or Yellowstone). A single system failure here represents a massive public health risk and potential park-wide closure. Localized Systems (Class 1 to 4): Systems serving under 3,300 people, often supporting remote ranger stations or campgrounds. While lower in raw numbers, these systems frequently lack backup sources, making them highly vulnerable to localized contamination.


2. Physical Water Source Vulnerability
The source of a water system dictates its environmental risk profile: Surface Water (SW) / Groundwater Under Direct Influence (GWUDI): Highly sensitive to immediate climate impacts, such as droughts, flash floods, agricultural runoff, and surface contamination. Groundwater (GW): Generally more stable, but highly susceptible to long-term geological contaminants (like arsenic) or persistent chemical infiltration (like PFAS).


3. Historical Compliance & Risk (SDWA Violations)
By analyzing historical violations, we can highlight systems with a track record of compliance issues, particularly those sensitive to climate stressors, providing park managers with a data-driven “early warning” system.

title: NPS Public Water System Analysis
text:
🗺️ Interactive System Explorer
Use the map below to explore public water systems across the National Park network. Filter layers using the top-right control to toggle park boundaries and water system classifications. Hover over any point to view the system name, exact population served, and size classification. Click on any park boundary to identify the official administrative unit name.
U.S. Environmental Protection Agency’s (EPA) Safe Drinking Water Information System (SDWIS) data downloaded from: https://echo.epa.gov/tools/data-downloads#drinkingwater (SDWA_PUB_WATER_SYSTEMS.csv) and joined with WSD using unique Public Water System Identification numbers.


Show R Code
# Load the tabular metadata (if needed for other plots, otherwise loaded here)
wsd_sdwa <- read_csv("data/wsd_sdwa_updated.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)

# 1. Load your local pre-simplified NPS park boundaries
nps_boundaries <- sf::st_read("data/nps_boundaries.geojson", quiet = TRUE) %>% 
  sf::st_transform(crs = 4326) 

# 2. Load the clean spatial data we exported
map_data <- sf::st_read("data/map_data.geojson", quiet = TRUE) %>% 
  sf::st_transform(crs = 4326)

# Define clean labels for the 11 population scales
pop_labels <- c(
  "<=100", "101-500", "501-1,000", "1,001-3,300", "3,301-10,000", 
  "10,001-50,000", "50,001-100,000", "100,001-250,000", 
  "250,001-500,000", "500,001-1,000,000", ">1,000,000"
)

# Define the master map-building function
build_nps_map <- function(data_input) {
# 3. Add back the hover text dynamically using your existing 'classification' column
map_data <- data_input %>%
  mutate(
    # Re-establish the factor levels so the legend stays in perfect numerical order
    POP_CAT_11_CODE = factor(POP_CAT_11_CODE, levels = as.character(1:11)),
    
    # Create the HTML list column on the fly
    hover_text = lapply(paste0(
      "<strong>System Name:</strong> ", PWS_NAME, "<br/>",
      "<strong>Classification:</strong> ", classification, "<br/>",
      "<strong>Population Served:</strong> ", scales::comma(POPULATION_SERVED_COUNT), "<br/>",
      "<strong>Population Range:</strong> ", POP_CAT_11_DESC
    ), HTML)
  )

# 4. Create palette mapping
pop_palette <- colorFactor(
  palette = "YlOrRd", 
  domain = map_data$POP_CAT_11_CODE
)

# Get unique classifications to build the interactive layers dynamically
classifications <- unique(map_data$classification)

# 5. Initialize the leaflet map
m <- leaflet() %>%
  addProviderTiles(providers$Esri.WorldTopoMap) %>% 
  setView(lng = -98.5795, lat = 39.8283, zoom = 4) %>%
  
  # Add National Park Boundaries
  addPolygons(
    data = nps_boundaries,
    fillColor = "#2a9d8f",             
    fillOpacity = 0.15,                
    color = "#1d3557",                 
    weight = 1.2,                      
    popup = ~UNIT_NAME,                
    group = "Park Boundaries"          
  )

# 6. Dynamically add a separate marker layer for each classification
for (cl in classifications) {
  sub_data <- map_data %>% filter(classification == cl)
  
  m <- m %>% addCircleMarkers(
    data = sub_data,
    lng = ~source_longitude, 
    lat = ~source_latitude,
    radius = 6,                      
    color = "#4a4a4a",               
    fillColor = ~pop_palette(POP_CAT_11_CODE), 
    fillOpacity = 0.8,
    stroke = TRUE,
    weight = 0.8,
    label = ~hover_text,
    labelOptions = labelOptions(
      style = list("font-weight" = "normal", padding = "3px 8px"),
      textsize = "13px",
      direction = "auto"
    ),
    group = cl # Assigns this subset of dots to its own toggle group
  )
}

# 7. Add map styling controls and the interactive legend
m %>%
  addLegend(
    pal = pop_palette, 
    values = map_data$POP_CAT_11_CODE, 
    opacity = 0.8, 
    title = "Population Served",
    labFormat = labelFormat(transform = function(x) pop_labels[as.numeric(x)]),
    position = "bottomright"
  ) %>%
  
  # Interactive Layer Toggles
  addLayersControl(
    overlayGroups = c("Park Boundaries", classifications),
    options = layersControlOptions(collapsed = FALSE),
    position = "topright"
  )
}

build_nps_map(map_data)

title: NPS Public Water System Analysis
section: Methodological Refinement: Allocating Park Visitation to Individual Water Systems
text:
Methodological Refinement: Allocating Park Visitation to Individual Water Systems
When evaluating public health exposure and infrastructure stress across national parks, relying solely on total park-level visitation can distort risk assessments. Large, multi-use park units, such as Grand Canyon, Yellowstone, or Yosemite, host millions of annual visitors distributed across dozens of geographically isolated water systems.
Assigning a park’s total visitor count to every water asset within its boundaries would severely over-penalize small, low-capacity systems (e.g., remote trailhead well systems or backcountry housing units) simply because they reside within a high-traffic park. Conversely, it underrepresents the true relative burden placed on major utility hubs that serve primary lodge and visitor center complexes.
To resolve this imbalance, we implement a Weighted Capacity Allocation Model to estimate the true System-Allocated Visitor Footprint.


🧮 Mathematical Framework
To distribute park-level visitor traffic across individual assets proportional to their designed supply responsibility, we calculate a system’s capacity ratio relative to the total NPS-managed infrastructure capacity within that park unit:

Park Infrastructure Capacity (\(\text{Pop}_{\text{park}}\)): We aggregate the Safe Drinking Water Act (SDWA) population served across all directly managed NPS water systems within a single park unit:

\[\text{Pop}_{\text{park}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Pop}_{\text{system}_i}\]

System Capacity Share (\(W_{\text{system}}\)): We establish the proportional capacity weight (\(W\)) for each system:

\[W_{\text{system}} = \frac{\text{Pop}_{\text{system}}}{\text{Pop}_{\text{park}}}\]

Allocated Annual Visitors (\(\text{Vis}_{\text{allocated}}\)): We apply this capacity weighting factor to the park’s 5-year average annual visitation (\(\text{Vis}_{\text{park}}\)):

\[\text{Vis}_{\text{allocated}} = W_{\text{system}} \times \text{Vis}_{\text{park}}\]



🎯 Key Assumptions & Operational Context

Design Capacity as a Demand Proxy: In SDWA reporting, POPULATION_SERVED_COUNT reflects peak design capacity or overnight service bounds. Using this value to weight visitor demand assumes that infrastructure investment naturally scales with human foot traffic across park sub-districts.
Granular Risk Scoring: By shifting from raw park totals to Vis_allocated, our System Priority Index (SPI) accurately scores Systemic Pressure at the individual asset level. This ensures capital improvement recommendations target systems experiencing genuine high-volume operational fatigue



Show R Code
system_visitor_allocation <- wsd_with_5yr_visitors %>%
  # Ensure population and visitation numbers are numeric and non-NA
  mutate(
    POPULATION_SERVED_COUNT = coalesce(POPULATION_SERVED_COUNT, 0),
    Avg_Annual_Visits_5Yr   = coalesce(Avg_Annual_Visits_5Yr, 0)
  ) %>%
  
  # Group by Park to calculate total park-level infrastructure capacity
  group_by(park_name) %>%
  mutate(
    park_total_pop_served = sum(POPULATION_SERVED_COUNT, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  
  # Calculate System Ratio & Allocated Visitor Footprint
  mutate(
    # Ratio of system capacity to overall park water capacity
    # (Avoid division by zero if a park has 0 recorded population)
    system_pop_ratio = if_else(
      park_total_pop_served > 0, 
      POPULATION_SERVED_COUNT / park_total_pop_served, 
      0
    ),
    
    # Fraction of park visitors allocated to this specific system
    system_allocated_visitors = round(system_pop_ratio * Avg_Annual_Visits_5Yr, 0)
  )
# 2. Extract and format the Top 10 systems by allocated visitors
top_10_allocated <- system_visitor_allocation %>%
  filter(!is.na(pws_number)) %>%
  distinct(pws_number, .keep_all = TRUE) %>%
  arrange(desc(system_allocated_visitors)) %>%
  slice(1:10) %>%
  mutate(
    # Multiply by 100 so 0.25 becomes 25.0
    system_pop_ratio = system_pop_ratio * 100
  ) %>%
  select(
    `System ID`                = pws_number,
    `System Name`              = PWS_NAME,
    `Park Name`                = park_name,
    `System Capacity Share`    = system_pop_ratio,
    `Total Park Visitors`      = Avg_Annual_Visits_5Yr,
    `Allocated Visitor Load`   = system_allocated_visitors
  )
# 3. Design the Flextable
ft_allocated <- flextable(top_10_allocated) %>%
  theme_vanilla() %>%
  
  # Format percentages (now scaled) and big numbers cleanly
  colformat_double(j = "System Capacity Share", digits = 1, suffix = "%") %>%
  colformat_double(j = c("Total Park Visitors", "Allocated Visitor Load"), big.mark = ",", digits = 0) %>%
  
  # Header Styling
  bg(bg = "#2a9d8f", part = "header") %>%
  color(color = "white", part = "header") %>%
  bold(part = "header") %>%
  align(align = "center", part = "header") %>%
  
  # Body Alignment
  align(j = c("System ID", "System Capacity Share", "Total Park Visitors", "Allocated Visitor Load"), align = "center", part = "body") %>%
  align(j = c("System Name", "Park Name"), align = "left", part = "body") %>%
  
  # Highlight the key output column with a light background
  bg(j = "Allocated Visitor Load", bg = "#f4f9f8", part = "body") %>%
  bold(j = "Allocated Visitor Load", part = "body") %>%
  
  autofit()

# Render table
ft_allocated


System IDSystem NamePark NameSystem Capacity ShareTotal Park VisitorsAllocated Visitor LoadVA6059523GREAT FALLS PARKGeorge Washington Memorial Parkway100.0%40,157,20640,157,206NJ1331001GATEWAY NRA SANDY HOOKGateway National Recreation Area100.0%9,596,2139,596,213CA2110009SLIDE RANCHGolden Gate National Recreation Area49.5%15,448,3187,642,852KY0072713CUMBERLAND GAP NHP/HENSLEYCumberland Gap National Historical Park100.0%7,425,9627,425,962TN0000270GREAT SMOKY MTNS NATIONAL PARK HQGreat Smoky Mountains National Park26.1%23,517,2576,148,881AZ0410703SAGUARO NATIONAL PARKSaguaro National Park98.4%5,108,0565,024,318AZ0403702GRAND CANYON NATIONAL PARKGrand Canyon National Park99.7%4,686,0104,672,491TN0001262CABLE MILL COMFORT STATIONGreat Smoky Mountains National Park16.7%23,517,2573,935,284TN0001263CADES COVE CAMPGROUNDGreat Smoky Mountains National Park16.7%23,517,2573,935,284MS0410023NATCHEZ TRACE PARKWAYNatchez Trace Parkway24.0%16,146,4153,877,180


Reading the System Capacity Share Plot:

Bar Length (Total Park Load): The total length of each horizontal bar represents the park’s overall 5-year average annual visitation.
Color Segments (Individual Systems): Each distinct colored segment within a bar corresponds to an individual public water system operating within that park unit. The width of a segment reflects its capacity share ratio—the proportion of total park water capacity managed by that specific system.

Solid, single-color bars indicate parks reliant on a centralized water system carrying 100% of the operational footprint (high single-point failure risk).
Multi-segmented bars indicate a decentralized network of smaller systems sharing total park visitor demand (distributed operational risk).

Interactive Exploration: Hover over any colored segment to inspect the exact system name, its percentage capacity share, and its calculated Allocated Annual Visitor Load.



Show R Code
# 1. Extract the unique park names directly from your Top 10 table
# Note: Uses `Park Name` if extracted after select(), or `park_name` if extracted before select()
target_parks <- unique(top_10_allocated$`Park Name`)

# 2. Filter system_visitor_allocation for systems within those target parks
plot_data <- system_visitor_allocation %>%
  filter(park_name %in% target_parks) %>%
  mutate(
    # Fallback hierarchy: PWS_NAME -> water_system_name -> pws_number -> Unregistered
    clean_system_name = coalesce(PWS_NAME, water_system_name, pws_number, "Unregistered System")
  )

# 3. Build plot matching the top 10 table
top_parks_plot <- ggplot(plot_data, aes(
  x = reorder(park_name, Avg_Annual_Visits_5Yr), 
  y = system_allocated_visitors, 
  fill = clean_system_name,
  text = paste0("<b>Park:</b> ", park_name, "<br>",
                "<b>System:</b> ", clean_system_name, "<br>",
                "<b>Share:</b> ", round(system_pop_ratio * 100, 1), "%<br>",
                "<b>Allocated Visitors:</b> ", scales::comma(system_allocated_visitors))
)) +
  geom_col(position = "stack", color = "white", linewidth = 0.2) +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::label_comma()) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    legend.position = "none",
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14)
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "System Capacity Share Across Top 10 Parks",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Allocated Annual Visitors"
  )

# 4. Render interactive plot
ggplotly(top_parks_plot, tooltip = "text") 








Priority Risk Framework: System Priority Index (SPI)
To move beyond isolated metrics, we synthesize structural system features with human foot traffic into a unified System Priority Index (SPI). The SPI evaluates each directly managed water asset across three foundational risk dimensions yielding a scaled composite score from 0 to 100::
1. Infrastructural Load (35% Weight): Local population capacity served (`POPULATION_SERVED_COUNT`). 2. Systemic Pressure (35% Weight): The proportional 5-year visitor footprint (`system_allocated_visitors`). 3. Environmental Exposure (30% Weight): Physical water source vulnerability, penalizing surface water systems due to direct climate susceptibility (e.g., drought sensitivity, algae blooms, and runoff contamination) compared to protected groundwater aquifers.
\(\text{SPI} = \left( 0.35 \cdot S_{\text{load}} + 0.35 \cdot S_{\text{pressure}} + 0.30 \cdot S_{\text{source}} \right) \times 20\)

Risk Categorization

Tier 1: Critical Priority (\(\text{SPI} \ge 80\)): High-capacity surface water or highly stressed groundwater systems requiring prioritized capital investment and proactive water quality monitoring.
Tier 2: High Priority (\(60 \le \text{SPI} < 80\)): Systems experiencing substantial visitor load or moderate environmental exposure.
Tier 3: Moderate Priority (\(40 \le \text{SPI} < 60\)): Lower-capacity systems operating under typical baseline conditions.
Tier 4: Low Priority (\(\text{SPI} < 40\)): Small groundwater systems or third-party purchased connections with low visitor demand.



Show R Code
spi_dataset <- system_visitor_allocation %>%
  mutate(
    # 1. Map fine-grained source_type into standardized Environmental Risk Categories
    source_category = case_when(
      source_type %in% c("river/stream", "lake/reservoir", "ocean", "rainwater") ~ "Surface / Direct Surface Water",
      source_type %in% c("spring", "reclaimed")                                  ~ "Spring / Reclaimed Water",
      source_type %in% c("deep groundwater", "shallow groundwater", 
                         "unknown groundwater") | is.na(source_type)            ~ "Groundwater (All Depths)",
      source_type == "purchased/concessioner"                                   ~ "Purchased / Concessioner",
      TRUE                                                                      ~ "Groundwater (All Depths)"
    ),
    
    # 2. Assign Granular Environmental Risk Scores (1 to 5)
    score_source = case_when(
      # Highest vulnerability: Direct exposure to weather, runoff, and drought
      source_type %in% c("river/stream", "lake/reservoir", "ocean") ~ 5,
      source_type == "rainwater"                                   ~ 4.5,
      
      # Moderate-High vulnerability: Highly variable flow / complex treatment
      source_type == "spring"                                      ~ 4,
      source_type == "reclaimed"                                   ~ 3.5,
      
      # Moderate-Low vulnerability: Soil-filtered aquifers
      source_type == "shallow groundwater"                         ~ 3,
      source_type %in% c("deep groundwater", "unknown groundwater") ~ 2,
      
      # Lowest direct NPS environmental exposure (third-party delivery)
      source_type == "purchased/concessioner"                      ~ 1,
      
      # Fallback for NA
      TRUE                                                         ~ 2
    ),
    
    # Dimension 2: Local Capacity Score (Quintile Bins 1-5)
    score_load = ntile(POPULATION_SERVED_COUNT, 5),
    
    # Dimension 3: Systemic Pressure Score (Quintile Bins 1-5)
    score_pressure = ntile(system_allocated_visitors, 5),
    
    # Calculate Composite SPI Score (0 - 100 Scale)
    spi_score = round(((0.35 * score_load) + (0.35 * score_pressure) + (0.30 * score_source)) * 20, 1),
    
    # Assign Priority Risk Tiers
    priority_tier = case_when(
      spi_score >= 80 ~ "Tier 1: Critical Priority",
      spi_score >= 60 ~ "Tier 2: High Priority",
      spi_score >= 40 ~ "Tier 3: Moderate Priority",
      TRUE            ~ "Tier 4: Low Priority"
    ),
    
    # Clean system display name
    clean_system_name = coalesce(PWS_NAME, water_system_name, pws_number, "Unregistered System")
  )

# 2. Build Interactive Priority Plot
spi_plot <- ggplot(spi_dataset, aes(
  x = system_allocated_visitors,
  y = spi_score,
  color = source_category,
  size = POPULATION_SERVED_COUNT,
  text = paste0(
    "<b>System:</b> ", clean_system_name, "<br>",
    "<b>Park:</b> ", park_name, "<br>",
    "<b>SPI Score:</b> ", spi_score, " (", priority_tier, ")<br>",
    "<b>Source Type:</b> ", source_category, "<br>",
    "<b>Allocated Visitors:</b> ", scales::comma(system_allocated_visitors), "<br>",
    "<b>Population Served:</b> ", scales::comma(POPULATION_SERVED_COUNT)
  )
)) +
  # Add risk tier threshold reference line
  geom_hline(yintercept = 80, linetype = "dashed", color = "#e76f51", alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.75) +
  scale_x_log10(labels = scales::label_comma()) + # Log-scale x-axis to separate clusters
  scale_color_manual(values = c(
  "Surface / Direct Surface Water" = "#d90429", # Dark Red (High Risk)
  "Spring / Reclaimed Water"       = "#f77f00", # Orange (Moderate-High Risk)
  "Groundwater (All Depths)"       = "#2a9d8f", # Teal (Low Risk)
  "Purchased / Concessioner"       = "#e9c46a"  # Yellow (External)
)) +
  scale_size_continuous(range = c(2, 9), guide = "none") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14),
    legend.title = element_text(face = "bold")
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "System Priority Index (SPI) vs. Visitor Footprint",
    x = "Allocated Visitor Footprint (Log Scale)",
    y = "System Priority Index Score (0-100)",
    color = "Water Source Type"
  )

# Render interactive Plotly graphic
ggplotly(spi_plot, tooltip = "text")
